text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
We have been walking through the list of what the Kohathites carried on their shoulders through the wilderness. We have looked closely at the Ark of the Covenant and all that it contained. We have studied the Table of Showbread in detail; its construction, its twelve loaves, its frankincense, and what it means for us as new creation believers to carry it today. Now we come to the third item on the list. Numbers 4:9 says: “They are to take a blue cloth and cover the lampstand that is for light, together with its lamps, its wick trimmers and trays, and all its jars for the olive oil used to supply it.” The lampstand. The menorah. The one source of light in the entire Holy Place. Today we look at what it was, how it was made, and how the priests covered it and prepared it for the Kohathites to carry. The spiritual meaning we will study tomorrow.

The lampstand is described in precise detail in Exodus 25:31-40. It was made entirely of pure gold; not wood covered with gold like the table or the ark,
but solid hammered gold from the base to the tip of every branch. The Hebrew word for this kind of work is miqshah, meaning hammered work; beaten into shape by the hands of a craftsman, not cast in a mold. God gave Moses the design on the mountain, and even then, Moses found it so difficult to picture that God showed him a fiery model of it. The entire lampstand, together with all its utensils, was made from one talent of pure gold; approximately 34 kilograms of gold. At today’s prices, the gold alone would be worth 47 crores. This was not a modest piece of furniture. It was the most expensive single object in the entire tabernacle.

The lampstand had one central shaft rising from a base, with six branches extending from it; three on the left and three on the right. Together with the central shaft, this made seven lamps in total. The number seven in Scripture speaks of completeness and fullness. All seven lamps faced forward,
toward the front of the Holy Place, so that the light fell in one direction.
There were no windows in the tabernacle. Without the lampstand, the Holy Place was in complete darkness. The lampstand was the only source of light for the priests as they carried out their work before God. It burned continuously tended twice every day, morning and evening, by the priests who trimmed the wicks and refilled the oil.

The entire lampstand was decorated with almond blossoms. Each of the six branches had three cups shaped like almond flowers, each with a bud and a petal; three cups per branch, making nine on each branch. The central shaft had four almond flower cups. In total there were twenty-two almond blossoms on the lampstand, along with connecting knobs beneath each pair of branches where they joined the shaft. Every detail was made of one hammered piece; the base, the shaft, the branches, the knobs, the flowers, all one continuous piece of beaten gold. Nothing was separate. Nothing was attached afterward.
It was all one.

The almond was the first tree in Israel to blossom after winter. When everything else was still bare and cold, the almond tree would suddenly burst into white blossom; the first sign that life was returning. The Hebrew word for almond, shaqed, means to watch or to hasten, because the almond hastens to blossom before all others. The lampstand was covered from base to branch tip with this flower; the flower of new life, of watching, of hastening. In addition to the lampstand itself, several utensils were used to tend it. The wick trimmers; also called snuffers, were golden tongs used to trim the wicks when they became too long or too charred. The trays caught the trimmed wicks so that nothing unclean fell down. The oil jars held the pure olive oil that kept the flames burning. All of these utensils were made of pure gold as well.

The lampstand stood on the south side of the Holy Place, directly opposite the Table of Showbread, which stood on the north side. The two pieces of furniture faced each other across the Holy Place; the bread on the right and the light on the left as the priests entered. Between them, further in toward the veil, stood the Golden Altar of Incense. The lampstand gave light to everything in the room. Without it, the showbread could not be seen. Without it, the golden altar could not be approached. Without it, the priests could not serve. The light made everything else in the Holy Place visible and functional. Where the lampstand was not, nothing else could work.

When the camp was ready to move, Aaron and his sons came into the Holy Place to prepare the lampstand for the journey. The covering of the lampstand was different from the coverings used for the Ark and the Table. Those items had multiple layers of covering, each layer a different colour with its own significance. The lampstand was covered with careful precision. First, the priests took a blue cloth and spread it over the entire lampstand; over its base, its shaft, its seven branches, and all the almond blossoms from the bottom to the top. The blue cloth covered everything.

Then, over the blue cloth, the priests wrapped the lampstand and all its utensils together in a covering of fine leather; translated in various versions as porpoise skin, dugong skin, or sea-cow skin. This was the same tough, durable, waterproof outer covering used to protect the Ark and the Golden Altar during transport. It kept out the desert dust, the heat, and the rain. Under the leather was the blue cloth. Under the blue cloth was the gold. When the Kohathites came to carry it, all they saw was the dark, plain leather on the outside. The gold and the blue were completely hidden within.

Then the lampstand and all its wrapped utensils were placed on a carrying frame; a wooden bar or frame through which poles were inserted so that it could be lifted onto the shoulders of the Kohathites. Unlike the Ark and the Table, the lampstand had no rings of its own for carrying poles. It was placed as a bundle onto the frame, with everything tucked inside the leather covering. The Kohathites then lifted the frame onto their shoulders. The most expensive object in the tabernacle; 34 kilograms of hammered gold, carved with almond blossoms from base to tip; was carried through the desert wrapped in plain sea-cow leather, looking from the outside like nothing more than a rough bundle on a wooden frame.

There is something deeply instructive in this. The lampstand, which was the most ornate and costly piece in the entire tabernacle, was also the most completely hidden during the journey. No one walking past the Kohathite carrying it would have known what was inside that leather wrapping. The light that illuminated everything in the Holy Place was completely invisible to the outside world while it was being carried through the wilderness. Tomorrow we will study what all of this means; what the lampstand points to, and what it means that the congregation carries it on their shoulders today.

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, You are my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? (Psalm 27:1). You did not leave Your people in darkness. You placed a lampstand in the middle of the Holy Place so that nothing in Your presence would be unseen, and no priest would stumble in the dark while serving You. The lampstand burned morning and evening, tended carefully, never allowed to go out. We thank You that You are the God who provides light; not occasionally, not only in the easy seasons, but continuously. As we study this lampstand, open our eyes to see what it was truly showing us. And as we carry it on our shoulders through the wilderness of this age, may we carry it with the same care the priests used when they covered and prepared it; reverently, precisely, and with the deep knowledge of what is inside the covering. In Christ’s name, amen.
